records.ety: [OE. treten, OF. traitier, F. traiter, from L. tractare to draw violently, to handle, manage, treat, v. intens. from trahere, tractum, to draw. See Trace, v. t., and cf. Entreat, Retreat, Trait.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Treated; p. pr. & vb. n. Treating.]

defs: To handle; to manage; to use; to bear one's self toward; as, to treat prisoners cruelly; to treat children kindly.
To discourse on; to handle in a particular manner, in writing or speaking; as, to treat a subject diffusely.
To entertain with food or drink, especially the latter, as a compliment, or as an expression of friendship or regard; as, to treat the whole company.
To negotiate; to settle; to make terms for.
quotes: To treat the peace, a hundred senators Shall be commissioned.

defs: To care for medicinally or surgically; to manage in the use of remedies or appliances; as, to treat a disease, a wound, or a patient.
To subject to some action; to apply something to; as, to treat a substance with sulphuric acid.
To discourse; to handle a subject in writing or speaking; to make discussion; -- usually with of; as, Cicero treats of old age and of duties.
quotes: And, shortly of this story for to treat.

defs: To negotiate; to come to terms of accommodation; -- often followed by with; as, envoys were appointed to treat with France.
quotes: Inform us, will the emperor treat!

defs: To give a gratuitous entertainment, esp. of food or drink, as a compliment.

quotes: Bid him battle without further treat.

defs: An entertainment given as an expression of regard.
That which affords entertainment; a gratification; a satisfaction; as, the concert was a rich treat.
